label: Grouped-Query Attention
definition: An attention variant that lets several query heads share a single key and value projection instead of each head owning its own, shrinking the KV cache and the memory bandwidth that dominate inference, with little loss in quality.

body.text: Grouped-query attention is a small change to multi-head attention that pays off heavily at inference time. In standard multi-head attention, every one of the model's attention heads has its own query, key, and value projections. Grouped-query attention keeps a separate query projection per head but lets a group of query heads share one key and value projection. It sits on a spectrum between two extremes: with as many key and value heads as query heads it is ordinary multi-head attention, and with a single shared key and value head it becomes multi-query attention.

The reason this matters is the kv-cache. When a large language model generates text one token at a time, it stores the keys and values of every past token so it does not recompute them. The size of that cache, and the memory bandwidth needed to read it back on every decoding step, are the main bottleneck of autoregressive generation, not the raw arithmetic. By cutting the number of key and value heads, grouped-query attention shrinks the cache and the bandwidth proportionally, which speeds up generation and lets the model serve longer context windows and larger batches within the same memory budget.

Multi-query attention took this idea to its limit and was fast, but sharing a single key and value head across all queries noticeably hurt quality and made training less stable. Grouped-query attention recovers most of that quality by keeping a handful of groups, so each cluster of query heads still attends through its own keys and values. It is close enough to full multi-head attention in accuracy while capturing most of the speedup.

Because of this favorable trade, grouped-query attention has become a default in many modern open-weight models. It composes naturally with flash-attention, which optimizes how the attention computation moves through GPU memory, and with the kv-cache it is designed to slim down.

image.alt: Three attention layouts compared. Multi-head on the left gives every query column its own key and value. Grouped-query in the middle lets each group of query columns share one key and one value. Multi-query on the right has all query columns share a single key and value.
image.caption: Grouped-query attention (middle) is the compromise between multi-head attention (left), where every query head has its own key and value, and multi-query attention (right), where all heads share one key and value. By giving each group of query heads a shared key and value, it shrinks the KV cache and memory bandwidth that dominate inference while keeping quality close to full multi-head attention.
image.credit: Joshua Ainslie et al. (GQA paper, arXiv:2305.13245)
